steps.content:
A **subgraph** is a task that contains a whole pipeline of its own.

Subgraphs help you manage complex projects: bundle a section of work into a single node, hide its internals, and reuse that bundle wherever it makes sense.

This example pipeline already contains three subgraphs for you to explore.

The three highlighted tasks (**Data Preparation**, **Training**, and **Evaluation**) are each a subgraph.

They render like ordinary tasks on the canvas, but each one wraps an internal pipeline of its own.
**Double-click** the **Data Preparation** task to look inside.

You're now one level deep, inside the Data Preparation subgraph.

The **breadcrumbs** above the canvas show your current location in the pipeline tree. Clicking any crumb takes you back up to that level.

For broader navigation, the **Pipeline Structure** window in the left dock shows the full pipeline tree at a glance, with every subgraph expandable in place.

Clicking a node in the tree jumps you directly there, no double-clicking required.

**Input nodes** play a slightly different role inside a subgraph. At the top level they become pipeline parameters set at run submission, but here they become the subgraph's **arguments** on the parent task, fed by whatever the parent connects to (or sets as a static value).

**Output nodes** play the inverse role. At the top level they're the pipeline's results, captured at run completion. Inside a subgraph, they become the subgraph's **outputs** on the parent task, ready to be wired into downstream tasks at the level above.
Click **Root** in the breadcrumbs to return to the top level.

Subgraphs behave a little differently at runtime.

The subgraph itself doesn't execute any code, so it has no logs of its own. Instead, it shows an **aggregate status** that summarizes the state of its inner tasks. To inspect individual task logs in the run view, navigate into the subgraph the same way you did here.

Subgraphs can also be **nested** inside other subgraphs, and the same navigation and aggregation rules apply at every level.

Subgraphs work in the other direction too. **Unpacking** a subgraph flattens it back into its constituent tasks at the parent level.

This is useful when you want to edit a subgraph's internals inline, or when a bundle is no longer earning its keep.
Click the **Data Preparation** task once to select it.
With the subgraph selected, open the **Node** menu in the top bar and choose **Unpack Subgraph**.
Data Preparation is gone. Its inner tasks, **Generate** and **Split**, are now sitting at the top level, already wired into the rest of the pipeline.

Now let's repackage them.

Hold **Cmd** (or **Ctrl** on Windows) and click the Generate task, then the Split task to select both. Dragging a selection box across them on empty canvas works just as well.
A floating toolbar appears above your selection. Click **Create Subgraph**, give the new subgraph a name, and confirm.

You've built a new subgraph from scratch.

Reach for subgraphs whenever a section of your pipeline reads as a single logical step, or you find you are often repeating combinations of tasks. They keep the top level clean and turn complex workflows into reusable building blocks.
